section.title: Woes to the Scribes and Pharisees
section.sectionRationale: Jesus' last public words are a sustained exposure of the religious leaders' hypocrisy. He warns the crowds not to imitate them, pronounces seven woes on scribes and Pharisees who major on externals while neglecting justice, mercy, and faithfulness, and ends with a grieving lament over Jerusalem.
section.thesis: Religion that polishes the outside while the heart is full of greed and pride is condemned by God, who weighs justice, mercy, and faithfulness; yet even in judgment Jesus grieves over the city that would not be gathered, longing to shelter His people.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the warning against the leaders' hypocrisy and pride; the seven woes on the scribes and Pharisees; and the lament over Jerusalem.

units.label: Do not imitate them
units.note: they do not practice what they preach

verses.text: Then Jesus spoke to the crowds and to His disciples:
“The scribes and Pharisees sit in Moses’ seat.
So practice and observe everything they tell you. But do not do what they do, for they do not practice what they preach.
They tie up heavy, burdensome loads and lay them on men’s shoulders, but they themselves are not willing to lift a finger to move them.
All their deeds are done for men to see. They broaden their phylacteries and lengthen their tassels.
They love the places of honor at banquets, the chief seats in the synagogues,
the greetings in the marketplaces, and the title of ‘Rabbi’ by which they are addressed.
But you are not to be called ‘Rabbi,’ for you have one Teacher, and you are all brothers.
And do not call anyone on earth your father, for you have one Father, who is in heaven.
Nor are you to be called instructors, for you have one Instructor, the Christ.
The greatest among you shall be your servant.
For whoever exalts himself will be humbled, and whoever humbles himself will be exalted.

units.label: The seven woes
units.note: outside clean, inside full of greed

verses.text: Woe to you, scribes and Pharisees, you {{hypokrites|hypocrites}}! You shut the kingdom of heaven in men’s faces. You yourselves do not enter, nor will you let in those who wish to enter.
Woe to you, scribes and Pharisees, you hypocrites! You traverse land and sea to win a single convert, and when he becomes one, you make him twice as much a son of hell as you are.
Woe to you, blind guides! You say, ‘If anyone swears by the temple, it means nothing; but if anyone swears by the gold of the temple, he is bound by his oath.’
You blind fools! Which is greater: the gold, or the temple that makes it sacred?
And you say, ‘If anyone swears by the altar, it means nothing; but if anyone swears by the gift on it, he is bound by his oath.’
You blind men! Which is greater: the gift, or the altar that makes it sacred?
So then, he who swears by the altar swears by it and by everything on it.
And he who swears by the temple swears by it and by the One who dwells in it.
And he who swears by heaven swears by God’s throne and by the One who sits on it.

Woe to you, scribes and Pharisees, you hypocrites! You pay tithes of mint, dill, and cumin. But you have disregarded the weightier matters of the law: justice, mercy, and faithfulness. You should have practiced the latter, without neglecting the former.
You blind guides! You strain out a gnat but swallow a camel.
Woe to you, scribes and Pharisees, you hypocrites! You clean the outside of the cup and dish, but inside they are full of greed and self-indulgence.
Blind Pharisee! First clean the inside of the cup and dish, so that the outside may become clean as well.
Woe to you, scribes and Pharisees, you hypocrites! You are like whitewashed tombs, which look beautiful on the outside but on the inside are full of dead men’s bones and every kind of impurity.
In the same way, on the outside you appear to be righteous, but on the inside you are full of hypocrisy and wickedness.
Woe to you, scribes and Pharisees, you hypocrites! You build tombs for the prophets and decorate the monuments of the righteous.
And you say, ‘If we had lived in the days of our fathers, we would not have been partners with them in shedding the blood of the prophets.’
So you testify against yourselves that you are the sons of those who murdered the prophets.
Fill up, then, the measure of the sin of your fathers.
You snakes! You brood of vipers! How will you escape the sentence of {{gehenna|hell}}?
Because of this, I am sending you prophets and wise men and teachers. Some of them you will kill and crucify, and others you will flog in your synagogues and persecute from town to town.
And so upon you will come all the righteous blood shed on earth, from the blood of righteous Abel to the blood of Zechariah son of Berechiah, whom you murdered between the temple and the altar.
Truly I tell you, all these things will come upon this generation.

units.label: Lament over Jerusalem
units.note: how often I have longed to gather you

verses.text: O Jerusalem, Jerusalem, who kills the prophets and stones those sent to her, how often I have longed to {{episynago|gather}} your children together, as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings, but you were unwilling!
Look, your house is left to you desolate.
For I tell you that you will not see Me again until you say, ‘Blessed is He who comes in the name of the Lord.’”

hypokrites.gloss: An actor behind a mask, a pretender. The refrain of the woes: the scribes and Pharisees perform righteousness for an audience while their hearts are full of greed and pride, a gap between the outward show and the inner reality that God sees and judges.

gehenna.gloss: Hell, named after the Valley of Hinnom outside Jerusalem, a place of burning and refuse and, in Israel's past, child sacrifice. Jesus uses it for the final judgment; He asks how those who make converts twice the sons of hell will themselves escape its sentence.

episynago.gloss: To gather together, as a hen gathers her chicks. It expresses Jesus' tender, maternal longing to shelter Jerusalem's children under His wings; the tragedy is not that He was unwilling but that they were.

basic.context: In His final public address, Jesus warns the crowds and disciples about the scribes and Pharisees: they teach God's law but do not live it, loading burdens on others while seeking honor for themselves. He forbids His followers to crave titles and to lord it over others. Then He pronounces seven woes, exposing leaders who shut the kingdom, chase converts, split hairs over oaths, tithe herbs while ignoring justice and mercy, and look clean outside but are corrupt within. He ends grieving over Jerusalem, longing to gather her, yet warning her house is left desolate.
basic.hermeneutics: These woes are not a blanket attack on a people but a prophetic indictment of a particular hypocrisy that can infect any religion, including ours. Notice the recurring contrast of outside and inside, and the weightier matters, justice, mercy, faithfulness, against ceremonial trivia. The closing lament shows the heart behind the severity: judgment grieves the Judge.
basic.originalLanguages: The refrain names the leaders hypokritai, masked actors. Jesus warns of gehenna, hell, the fire of final judgment. And He laments that He longed to episynago, to gather Jerusalem's children like a hen her chicks, but they were unwilling.
basic.typology: Jesus stands in the line of the prophets who denounced hollow worship, but as more than a prophet, sending His own messengers. The hen gathering her chicks echoes God sheltering Israel under His wings.
basic.christ: Jesus is the one Teacher and Instructor, the Christ, the true and humble servant-leader against the proud. He is the sender of prophets, the judge who pronounces the woes, and the grieving Savior who longs to gather His people under His wings.
basic.god: God sees past every performance to the heart and weighs justice, mercy, and faithfulness above outward show. He is patient and pleading, longing to gather, yet just; persistent rejection leaves a house desolate.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 29 and Micah 6 weigh true worship against externals. Luke 11 gives parallel woes. Psalm 91 pictures refuge under God's wings. Psalm 118 supplies the closing blessed is He who comes.
basic.oneStory: Jesus' last public words expose a religion of appearances and defend the weightier matters of the heart, warning that God is not mocked by performance. Yet the severity ends in tears: the Judge longs to gather the very city that kills the prophets. The passion is near, and the One who weeps over Jerusalem will soon be crucified by her leaders, gathering His scattered people to Himself through the cross.

context.literary: This is Jesus' final public discourse before the private Olivet Discourse, and it closes His temple ministry. It gathers the conflict of chapters 21 and 22 into a sustained indictment structured around seven woes, moving from the leaders' pride to their coming judgment, and it ends with a lament that transitions to the prediction of the temple's destruction in chapter 24.
context.historical: Sitting in Moses' seat meant teaching with Moses' authority. Phylacteries were small boxes with Scripture worn on the arm and forehead; tassels were commanded fringes; both were being enlarged for display. Elaborate oath distinctions let people appear bound while evading obligation. Tithing garden herbs was scrupulous piety. Whitewashed tombs were freshly painted before Passover so pilgrims would not be defiled by touching them unawares.
context.canonical: The woes echo the prophetic critique of empty ritual, Isaiah 1 and 29, Micah 6:8, justice, mercy, and walking humbly. Abel to Zechariah spans the Hebrew canon from Genesis to Chronicles, all the martyred righteous. The gathering hen recalls God's wings in Deuteronomy 32:11 and Psalm 91:4, and the closing line quotes Psalm 118:26, tying back to the entry and forward to a future welcome.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To expose and condemn religious hypocrisy that prizes appearance over the heart and neglects justice, mercy, and faithfulness, to warn of judgment, and to reveal the grieving love of Christ who longs to gather even those who reject Him.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The woes describe and condemn specific sins of the leaders, and they prescribe by warning: do not do what they do. The positive commands, be a servant, humble yourself, practice justice and mercy, are directly prescriptive for all Jesus' followers.

debates.question: Does Jesus condemn the leaders' teaching, their practice, or both?

views.label: Chiefly their hypocritical practice
views.case: Jesus tells the crowds to observe what the leaders teach from Moses' seat but not to imitate their deeds; the indictment falls on the gap between their words and their lives, their pride and neglect of the heart, not on the law of Moses itself.

views.label: Also their distorted, burdensome teaching
views.case: The woes target their blind oath-casuistry, their converts becoming sons of hell, and their straining gnats while swallowing camels, so their teaching too is corrupted where tradition and evasion displace justice, mercy, and faithfulness.

debates.bearing: Both hold that God weighs the heart and the weightier matters of the law; where the leaders faithfully pass on God's word it is to be heeded, but their hypocrisy and their evasive additions are condemned together.

study.originalLanguages: Seven times the word falls: hypokritai, masked actors who play at righteousness before an audience while greed and pride fill the heart. Jesus strips away the performance, contrasting the polished outside with the corrupt inside, the tithed herbs with the neglected weightier matters of justice, mercy, and faithfulness. He warns that those who make converts twice the sons of gehenna, hell, will not themselves escape its sentence. Yet the discourse ends not in fury but in grief: how often I have longed to episynago, to gather your children as a hen gathers her chicks, but you were unwilling. The severity and the sorrow belong to the same heart, the heart of the Christ who will die to gather a scattered people.

typology.type: The prophet who indicts hollow worship
typology.fulfillment: Jesus in the line of Isaiah and Micah, yet greater, the one Teacher who sends His own prophets, Micah 6:8.
God sheltering His people under His wings, embodied in Christ's longing to gather Jerusalem, Deuteronomy 32:11, Psalm 91:4.

typology.type: The blood of the righteous from Abel onward
typology.fulfillment: The martyrs whose blood culminates at the cross, where the righteous Son is slain and yet secures redemption, Hebrews 12:24.

study.christ: Jesus is the true and humble Teacher against whom the proud leaders are measured, the one Instructor, the Christ, who alone deserves the honor they crave. He speaks as the prophets did, but as their Lord, for He is the one sending prophets and wise men to a generation that will kill and crucify them. He pronounces the woes as Judge, yet His last word over Jerusalem is a lament, revealing a heart that longs to gather her children under His wings as a hen shelters her chicks. That longing will carry Him to the cross, where the grieving Savior gathers His scattered people, and He promises that Jerusalem will yet greet Him with blessed is He who comes in the name of the Lord.
study.god: The God revealed here cannot be deceived by performance; He sees the inside of the cup and the whitewashed tomb, and He weighs justice, mercy, and faithfulness above scrupulous trivia. He is patient and pleading, longing to gather His people as a mother bird shelters her young, and slow to abandon them. Yet He is also just: persistent, hardened rejection of His messengers, and finally of His Son, leaves a house desolate and brings judgment on a generation. His severity and His sorrow are not at odds; both flow from a holy love that will neither excuse sin nor stop yearning for the sinner.

crossReferences.note: To do justice, love mercy, and walk humbly, the weightier matters the leaders neglected.
A people who honor God with their lips while their hearts are far, the root of the hypocrisy.
God covering His people under His wings, behind the image of the gathering hen.
Blessed is He who comes in the name of the LORD, Jerusalem's future welcome of her King.

study.oneStory: Jesus' final public words tear the mask off a religion of appearances and defend the weightier matters of justice, mercy, and faithfulness, warning that God is never mocked by performance. But the thunder of the woes breaks into tears: the Judge longs to gather the city that kills her prophets, as a hen gathers her chicks. That longing leads straight to the cross, where the grieving Savior is slain by the very leaders He indicts, and yet gathers a scattered people to Himself. The story presses toward the day Jerusalem will finally cry, blessed is He who comes in the name of the Lord.

quiz.q: What was the central charge in Jesus' woes against the scribes and Pharisees?

options.text: Hypocrisy: they polished the outside while their hearts were full of greed and pride.
options.note: The repeated word hypocrites and the outside-inside contrast define the woes.

options.text: They taught the law of Moses to the people.
options.note: Jesus said to observe what they teach from Moses' seat; the fault was their hypocritical lives, not teaching the law.

options.text: They were too strict in keeping God's commandments.
options.note: They were scrupulous about trivia but neglected justice and mercy; the problem was misplaced emphasis, not excess obedience.
They were intensely religious; the problem was hypocrisy, not irreligion.

quiz.q: What are the weightier matters of the law the leaders neglected?

options.note: Verse 23 names these against the tithing of mint, dill, and cumin.

options.text: Careful tithing of even the smallest herbs.
options.note: Jesus said not to neglect the tithing, but the weightier matters are justice, mercy, and faithfulness, which this reverses.
Ritual precision is the lesser matter here; the weightier ones concern the heart and neighbor.

options.text: Wearing broad phylacteries and long tassels.
options.note: Those were the showy externals Jesus condemned, not the weightier matters.

quiz.q: How does Jesus want greatness expressed among His followers?

options.text: In humble service, since the one who humbles himself will be exalted.
options.note: Verses 11-12 set service and humility against the leaders' craving for honor.

options.text: In earning respected titles like Rabbi and teacher.
options.note: The opposite; Jesus warns against craving such titles, pointing to one Teacher.

options.text: In taking the chief seats and public greetings.
options.note: These are exactly the honors Jesus rebukes the leaders for loving.

options.text: In quietly avoiding all responsibility for others.
options.note: Greatness is active service of others, not withdrawal from them.

quiz.q: What does Jesus' lament over Jerusalem reveal?

options.text: A grieving love that longed to gather the city, even as it faced judgment for rejecting Him.
options.note: Verse 37 shows Jesus' tender longing, met by the city's unwillingness.

options.text: That Jesus had given up all concern for Jerusalem.
options.note: The lament shows deep concern and longing, not indifference; judgment grieves Him.

options.text: That the judgment on Jerusalem was unjust.
options.note: The judgment is just; the lament reveals sorrow, not injustice.

options.text: That Jerusalem had always welcomed God's messengers.
options.note: Jesus says the city kills the prophets and was unwilling to be gathered.

questions: Jesus condemned doing deeds to be seen by others. Where do you catch yourself performing your faith for an audience rather than living it before God?
The leaders tithed herbs but neglected justice and mercy. What small religious details might you be using to distract from weightier matters God is asking of you?
Jesus contrasts the clean outside with the corrupt inside. If God cleaned the inside of your cup first, what would He start with?
Greatness is service, and the humble will be exalted. Where is pride shaping your desire for recognition, and what would humble service look like instead?
Jesus wept over the city that rejected Him, longing to gather it. How does the grieving heart of Christ move you toward those who resist Him, including yourself in your resistance?

translationNotes.note: The BSB numbering goes from verse 13 to verse 15; verse 14, about devouring widows' houses and making long prayers, is not in the earliest manuscripts and appears as a footnote drawn from Mark 12:40 and Luke 20:47.

From Abel to Zechariah spans the martyred righteous across the Hebrew Scriptures, from Genesis to Chronicles, the last book in the Hebrew ordering, so the phrase means all the righteous blood ever shed.
Some manuscripts read simply your house is left to you; the word desolate makes explicit the coming abandonment and destruction of the temple, foretold in the next chapter.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Matthew 23:1-39. Verse text assembled by concatenating segment values in order; heading, line-break, and note markers were skipped. Verse 14 is omitted in the BSB (earliest manuscripts) and is carried in a translation note. No em or en dashes appear. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
